name: B.A. in Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study lays out a clear, well-ordered IS spine across eight labeled terms: foundational computing and web work (CS 100, IS 117, IS 218, IS 265, IS 247) progress into database, requirements, and UX-research courses (IS 331, IS 390, IS 375, IS 344), and culminate in management and advanced/capstone work (IS 455, IS 465, IS 491). Prerequisite depth (deepest chain of 7, with IS 465 terminal) confirms courses genuinely build on one another term over term with no abrupt jumps. The main weakness is that later years lean heavily on unlabeled placeholder buckets (Career Track, New Media/Business Specialization, and General Electives), which leaves part of the upper-level progression unspecified.
dimensions.suggestion: Attach a concrete example course list to each elective bucket (Career Track, Specialization, General) so the upper-division progression is as legible as the required IS core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and sensible: a deepest chain of 7 reflects a genuine multi-year build-up (e.g., IS 465 requires a statistical GER plus IS 265, IS 344, and IS 331/CS 331) without appearing over-constrained. Prerequisites are richly specified with explicit grade conditions and boolean logic. Only 3 of 28 referenced codes are missing from the catalog (missingCourseCount=3: GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), and all three are peripheral — a general-education category reference and two 1-credit professional-development seminars — rather than breaks in the core IS chain.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the three dangling codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) with the undergraduate catalog by either linking them to real course entries or replacing the GER placeholder with the specific requirement it points to.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data (learningOutcomes is null). For a catalog page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real finding: prospective students, advisors, and accreditors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do, and the plan of study alone does not substitute for explicit competency statements.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes (e.g., 5-8 measurable statements covering systems analysis, database design, UX, and IS management) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summed term credits (120) equal the stated total (120), so creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 — internally consistent with no missing or double-counted credits. Term loads are reasonable and steady (mostly 15-16 credits, with a lighter 12-credit final term), and the mix spans general education (English, math, natural science, history/humanities GERs), a substantial IS major core, and multiple elective categories. The score is held just below exemplary because the numerous unlabeled elective buckets make the precise gen-ed / major-core / elective split hard to verify even though the totals reconcile.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. general education vs. free/track electives) so the balance behind the reconciled 120 total is transparent at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan of study is present with clearly labeled terms and per-item credits, which aids readability. However, completeness and structure suffer: there is no program-level description or overview narrative at all, and several rows are parsing artifacts — GER references have their URLs split across multiple line items with null course codes and null credits (e.g., a 'Natural Science GER (http://...' row followed by a stray 'requirements/natural-science-ger/)' row). These broken fragments and the missing overview undercut an otherwise legible plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a concise program overview paragraph and clean up the fragmented GER rows so each requirement renders as a single, complete line rather than split URL fragments.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current IS practice well: two dedicated UX courses (IS 247 Designing the User Experience, IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX), hands-on web application development (IS 117, IS 218), database design (IS 331), requirements analysis and systems design (IS 390), IS management and business processes (IS 455), and an advanced course (IS 465) that explicitly introduces data analysis and data-science techniques. Career-track and New Media/Business specialization electives add contemporary flexibility. Modern gaps are that cybersecurity and cloud computing appear only implicitly (networking is limited to the introductory IT 120) and data analytics is only introductory rather than a named required strand.
dimensions.suggestion: Name current-practice courses in data analytics, cloud, and cybersecurity as explicit required or career-track options rather than leaving them to unlabeled elective buckets.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 25 sampled courses carry substantive, specific description excerpts that convey real topics and scope (e.g., IS 218's Web-based information systems, IS 331's database back-end functionality, IS 465's data-analysis and statistics from an IS perspective) rather than thin placeholders. The only blemish is a short, artifact-laden MATH 138 excerpt ending in stray text ('298 Jordan'); otherwise descriptions are informative and consistent.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unusually clearly and consistently: they include explicit grade conditions ('ENGL 102 with a grade of C or higher') and well-formed boolean logic ('(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'). Clarity is lightly reduced by a few category-level references (e.g., 'Statistical GER', 'History and Humanities GER 200 level') and the 3 dangling codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), which are resolvable but not spelled out as concrete courses.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes (5-8 measurable competency statements); none are currently present.
Add a program overview/description narrative to the catalog page, which currently has none.
Clean up presentation artifacts and reconcile the 3 dangling course codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), including the GER rows whose URLs are split across null-code line items.
Name current-practice courses in data analytics, cloud, and cybersecurity as explicit required or career-track options rather than leaving them implicit.
Label the elective buckets (General, Career Track, and New Media/Business Specialization) with concrete example course lists so the core-vs-elective split is transparent.
